title: Motor Oil on Car Upholstery
stainChemistry: Motor oil is a petroleum distillate containing base oil, additives (zinc, phosphorus, detergents), and accumulated engine contaminants (carbon, metal particles). On car upholstery, the petroleum base penetrates rapidly and the additives leave metallic residue. Used motor oil also contains polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) that add dark color and resist standard cleaning.
whyThisWorks: POG or amyl acetate provides the strongest solvent action against petroleum compounds on car upholstery. The 'like dissolves like' principle applies — petroleum solvents dissolve petroleum stains. Surfactant then removes the dissolved residue. For the metallic/carbon component, repeated solvent-surfactant cycles are needed. This is a difficult stain that may require professional treatment for complete removal.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply POG (Paint Oil Grease remover) directly to the stain. Tamp gently
2. If POG plateaus: apply amyl acetate for stronger solvent action
3. Apply NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) to emulsify remaining oil micro-droplets. Flush with cool water
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) for final pH restoration
warnings: Apply solvent BEFORE water — water spreads oil stains
Ensure adequate ventilation when using solvents — VOC fumes require extraction

steps: 1. Sprinkle baking soda or cornstarch on the stain to absorb surface oil — wait 10 min
2. Brush off powder with a clean cloth
3. Apply Dawn dish soap directly to the stain. Work gently into fabric
4. Rinse with warm water. Repeat soap application if stain persists
5. Air dry — do NOT use dryer until stain is completely gone
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 treatment rounds. Take to a professional dry cleaner — they have specialized solvents for oil-based stains
warnings: Never add water to a fresh oil stain first — water and oil don't mix, you will spread it
Heat from dryer permanently sets oil residue

neverDo: No chlorine bleach — damages automotive fabric
No excess water — soaks into seat foam and causes mold/mildew
fiberSensitivities: Automotive fabrics are usually synthetic (nylon/polyester) — chemically durable
Seat foam absorbs moisture — use minimum liquid, extract thoroughly
Test in hidden area (under seat) before visible treatment

escalation.when: If motor oil stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is motor oil on car upholstery, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Is the oil fresh or has it been washed/dried already?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
